questions.question_text: An SC trust account must be established as which type of account?
questions.correct_answer: A demand deposit account (checking)
questions.explanation: SC law requires trust accounts to be demand deposit accounts (checking accounts) with an insured financial institution authorized to do business in South Carolina.

questions.question_text: The name on an SC real estate trust account must include which term?
questions.correct_answer: Trust or escrow
questions.explanation: The account title must include the word 'trust' or 'escrow' in the brokerage firm's name. This clearly identifies the account as holding other people's money.

questions.question_text: A brokerage has three office locations. How many trust accounts are required?
questions.correct_answer: One central account may serve all offices
distractors: One per BIC at each location
questions.explanation: One central trust account may serve multiple offices. The brokerage may have as many accounts as desired, but only one is required if the BIC holds trust funds.

questions.question_text: Can an SC trust account be interest-bearing?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if fund owners agree in writing
distractors: No, trust accounts cannot earn interest
Only if approved by the SCREC
questions.explanation: Interest-bearing trust accounts are permitted if they provide for withdrawal on demand. The BIC/PMIC may keep interest only if the fund owner(s) agree in writing, with conspicuous language if in preprinted forms.

questions.question_text: A BIC wants to keep interest earned on a client's earnest money deposit. Under SC law, this requires:
questions.correct_answer: Written consent from the fund owner
questions.explanation: The BIC may keep interest on trust funds only with written agreement from the fund owner(s). If the agreement is in a preprinted form, the language about interest must be conspicuous.

questions.question_text: A licensee receives a cash earnest money deposit on Wednesday. By when must it be deposited into the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: By Friday (within 48 hours)
questions.explanation: Cash or certified funds must be deposited within 48 hours of receipt, excluding Saturdays, Sundays, and bank holidays. Wednesday receipt means deposit by Friday.

questions.question_text: When must an earnest money check be deposited into the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Within 48 hours after offer acceptance
distractors: Within 48 hours of receiving the check
Within 24 hours of receiving the check
At the time of contract ratification only
questions.explanation: Checks must be deposited within 48 hours after acceptance of the offer by the parties (excluding weekends and bank holidays). The timing is triggered by acceptance, not receipt of the check.

questions.question_text: A BIC keeps $200 of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank service charges. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a nominal amount for bank fees is allowed
distractors: No, any personal funds constitute commingling
Only if the amount is under $100
questions.explanation: A clearly identified nominal amount of BIC/PMIC funds may remain in the trust account to cover bank service charges or prevent zero-balance closure. This is the sole exception to the commingling prohibition.

questions.question_text: A BIC deposits a $5,000 personal check into the trust account to temporarily cover a shortfall. This constitutes:
questions.correct_answer: Commingling of personal and trust funds
distractors: An acceptable temporary loan to the account
questions.explanation: Depositing personal funds beyond a nominal amount for bank fees is commingling. The only exception is a clearly identified nominal amount to cover bank charges or prevent account closure.

questions.question_text: Rent received by a licensee who is directly employed by the property owner may be deposited into:
questions.correct_answer: An operating or similar account
questions.explanation: Per Section 40-57-136(C)(1)(b), rent received by a licensee directly employed by the owner may go into an operating account. However, advance rental deposits are ALWAYS trust funds regardless.

questions.question_text: Buyer and seller disagree about who should receive the earnest money after a contract falls through. The broker may release the funds when:
questions.correct_answer: Both parties agree in a separate written agreement
distractors: The broker decides based on contract terms
The listing agent directs the disbursement
questions.explanation: When funds are disputed, the broker may release them only upon: mutual written agreement, court order, mediator direction, or by filing an interpleader action and depositing funds with the court.

questions.question_text: A broker cannot resolve a dispute over earnest money between buyer and seller. What legal action can the broker take?
questions.correct_answer: File an interpleader action with the court
distractors: Return the funds to the buyer by default
Split the funds equally between parties
Hold the funds indefinitely until resolved
questions.explanation: An interpleader action allows the broker to deposit disputed funds with the court clerk and let the court decide. This protects the broker from liability while ensuring proper resolution.

questions.question_text: Once deposited in a trust account, transaction funds must remain there until:
questions.correct_answer: Consummation or termination of the transaction
questions.explanation: Transaction funds must stay in the trust account until consummation (closing) or termination of the transaction. Early withdrawal requires proper authorization per disputed funds procedures.

questions.question_text: A tenant's security deposit must remain in the PM trust account until:
questions.correct_answer: Termination of the lease
distractors: The tenant's first month of occupancy
questions.explanation: Security deposits must remain in trust until lease termination. Rental funds are disbursed per the management contract, but security deposits are held for the duration of the tenancy.

questions.question_text: A trust account check is returned NSF. Under SC law, this may be:
questions.correct_answer: Grounds for disciplinary action
distractors: A minor issue with no consequences
questions.explanation: Returned checks (NSF or closed account) from a trust account may be grounds for disciplinary action by the SCREC. The BIC is responsible for maintaining sufficient funds and proper accounting.

questions.question_text: How long must an SC BIC maintain trust account records?
questions.correct_answer: A minimum of 5 years
questions.explanation: SC law requires trust account records to be maintained for a minimum of five years. Records include journals, ledgers, canceled checks, deposit slips, and bank statements.

questions.question_text: A BIC uses computerized bookkeeping for trust account records. What additional requirement applies?
questions.correct_answer: Backup copies at a separate off-site location
distractors: Manual duplicate ledgers must also be kept
A third-party auditor must verify annually
questions.explanation: Computerized trust account records require backup copies at a separate off-site location. This ensures records survive even if the primary system is damaged or lost.

questions.question_text: The SCREC may audit a brokerage's trust account records:
questions.correct_answer: At any time without prior notice
distractors: Only after receiving a formal complaint
Only with 30 days advance written notice
questions.explanation: The SCREC can audit trust accounts at any time. The BIC must make all records available for inspection upon request by a Commission representative.

questions.question_text: Which records must be maintained for an SC real estate trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Journals, ledgers, deposit slips, and bank statements
distractors: Only the annual tax return for the account
questions.explanation: Required records include journals, ledgers, folios, client subaccounts, tenant accounts, canceled checks, deposit slips, and bank statements — all maintained for at least 5 years.

questions.question_text: An SCREC auditor requests trust account records from a BIC. The BIC has 4-year-old records available but claims older records were destroyed. Is this a violation?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, records must be kept for 5 years minimum
distractors: No, 3 years of records is sufficient
Only if the destroyed records were subpoenaed
questions.explanation: SC requires a minimum 5-year retention period for trust account records. Destroying records before the 5-year mark is a violation, and the BIC must furnish records to the Commission upon request.
